summary: Simon shares his personal toolkit for productivity and daily computer use, covering six categories of tools: time tracking (RescueTime), clipboard management (Ditto/Paste), password management (LastPass/KeePass), screen capturing (Snagit), blue light filtering (f.lux), and bookmark syncing (Xmarks). He emphasizes that these tools have genuinely improved his workflow and encourages readers to try them. The post reflects his practical, hands-on approach to optimizing work habits through technology. He notes this is part one of a series, with more tools to follow.
key_points: Time tracking tools like RescueTime provide honest feedback about productivity patterns and reveal where time actually goes
Clipboard managers are underrated utilities that become indispensable once you start using them for work
Password managers solve not just password problems but also the challenge of remembering which username was used where
Screen color temperature tools like f.lux can meaningfully improve sleep quality for late-night computer users
Visual communication tools like Snagit make explaining things remotely much more effective than text alone
money_quotes: The good thing about it is that this tool is not going to lie to you, it tells you the full truth.
And you probably think now 'I don't really use that kind app', but I can tell as soon you used it, you don't want to miss it anymore, give it a try.
So many websites, usernames, emails and passwords have to be remembered, it's not only about the passwords.
It's so much easier to understand if you add a speech bubble or a little text next to the thing which is important.
At 9PM, 10PM, or 3AM, you probably shouldn't be looking at the sun.
Although this sounds a bit over promised, it can improve your sleeping quality drastically.
key_insight: Small utility tools that run quietly in the background—tracking time, managing clipboards, storing passwords—compound into significant productivity gains when used consistently.
